data.title: Musk’s Terafab taps Applied Materials, Tokyo Electron, Lam Research in chip push: report
data.description:
Elon Musk’s lieutenants have reportedly reached out to chip industry suppliers including Applied Materials (AMAT), Tokyo Electron Ltd. and Lam Research Corp. (LRCX) for his envisioned Terafab, early steps in an audacious and likely arduous attempt to break into the production of cutting-edge chips.

Staff working for the joint venture between Tesla (TSLA) and SpaceX (SPACE) have sought price quotes and delivery times for an array of chipmaking gear, people familiar with the matter said.

In past weeks, they’ve contacted makers of photomasks, substrates, etchers, depositors, cleaning devices, testers, and other tools, according to the people.

The ​goal is ​to begin silicon manufacturing ​by 2029 and then ‌scale up, Bloomberg added.

Musk's representatives have asked for speedy price estimates while providing minimal information about the products, and Musk wants to move at "light speed," according to the report.

Musk launched Terafab ‌in March, and Intel (INTC) last week joined the Terafab project alongside the billionaire's companies SpaceX (SPACE), xAI (X.AI), and Tesla (TSLA) to "help refactor silicon fab technology."

data.title: Amazon-backed X-Energy seeks $800M in IPO listing
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN)-backed nuclear startup X-energy (XE) has filed for a U.S. initial public offering targeting up to $800M.

The Maryland-based company plans to sell roughly 42.9M shares priced between $16 and $19 each, implying potential top-end proceeds of about $814M, its SEC filing showed. The company is targeting a valuation close to $7.5B.

Backed by Amazon (AMZN), which previously led a $500M funding round, X-energy is positioning itself to benefit from surging electricity demand driven by AI data centers and broader electrification trends. The e-commerce giant has also committed to purchasing up to 5 gigawatts of nuclear power from the company by 2039.

The listing, expected on Nasdaq under the ticker “XE,” is being led by major banks, including JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, and Moelis.

Founded in 2009, X-energy develops small modular reactors ((SMRs)) and advanced nuclear fuel, aiming to deliver safer and more cost-effective alternatives to traditional nuclear plants.

X-Energy had earlier pursued a public listing through a SPAC backed by Ares Management in 2023, but ultimately scrapped those plans due to unfavorable market conditions.

Cathie Wood-led ARK Investment Management (ARKK) and its affiliates have expressed interest in purchasing up to $105 million worth of shares in X-Energy’s planned IPO, according to a Reuters report.

data.title: Traws outlines $60M financing while targeting flu challenge study this summer and runway into Q1 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Traws Pharma (TRAW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • "This morning, Traws announced a private financing of $60 million," said CEO Dr. Iain Dukes, adding that the capital "positions Traws to advance the flu program through a human challenge study this summer while providing access to additional capital as we achieve further key milestones." (CEO, Secretary & Director Iain Dukes)
  • "We will prioritize development of tivoxavir as a once-monthly prophylactic agent for influenza prevention," Dukes said, describing tivoxavir marboxil as "an exciting next-generation investigational influenza antiviral" and adding, "We believe tivoxavir is well positioned to become a best-in-class once-monthly oral prophylactic agent with additional potential for pandemic flu, including H5N1 bird flu." (CEO Dukes)
  • Dukes said the company intends to start the U.K. challenge study after regulatory clearance: "We intend to initiate the study this summer once we have approval received from the Medicines and Healthcare Products and Regulatory Agency... in the U.K." He also highlighted formulation work: "Data from preclinical studies show a 30% increase in exposure with this new formulation" and said the company is running "a Phase I bridging study in Australia to confirm the extended exposure." (CEO Dukes)
  • "FDA recently informed us that our IND filing has been placed on clinical hold due to concerns for the toxicology data package," Dukes said, adding, "We are actively engaging with the FDA to address its concerns and resolve the clinical hold as expeditious as possible with the goal of advancing the program in the U.S. in late 2026." (CEO Dukes)
  • "This morning, Traws announced the completion of a private financing that provides up to $60 million in potential gross proceeds," CFO Charles Parker said, detailing that the private placement includes "$10 million upfront" plus milestone-based and common warrants, and adding, "Based on our current plans, the company believes that its current cash balance, including net proceeds from the offering and milestone-based warrants, if fully exercised, is sufficient to support planned expenses into Q1 2027." (Chief Financial Officer Charles Parker)

Outlook

  • Dukes tied the near-term program path to two clinical activities: "We are in the process of conducting a Phase I bridging study in Australia" and said the Phase IIa prophylaxis challenge study will be run "at hVIVO in the U.K." with the trial "Starting in June." (CEO Dukes)
  • On regulatory timing risk in the U.K., Dukes said, "Our package has been submitted to MHRA. They are now under a 30-day clock to review the package that we have sent," while also noting, "It is frequently the case that these regulatory agents come to different conclusions based on identical toxicology packages submitted." (CEO Dukes)
  • On U.S. timing, Dukes reiterated the dependency on lifting the hold: "Our IND filing has been placed on clinical hold" and the goal is "advancing the program in the U.S. in late 2026." (CEO Dukes)
  • A quarter-over-quarter outlook comparison was not possible because the prior-quarter transcript was not provided beyond stating it was not available.

Financial Results

  • Parker reported liquidity at year-end: "As of December 31, 2025, Traws had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of approximately $3.8 million compared to $21.3 million as of December 31, 2024." (CFO Parker)
  • Parker reported revenue and its main driver: "Revenue for the year-ended December 31, 2025, was $2.8 million compared to $226,000 for the same period in 2024," and said the increase was attributable to "$2.7 million in deferred revenue recognized as revenue in the second quarter related to the mutual termination of a licensing agreement associated with our legacy oncology program in April of 2025." (CFO Parker)
  • Parker reported operating expense changes: "Research and development expense... totaled $12.1 million compared to $12.8 million" and "General and administrative expense... totaled $8.5 million compared to $12.3 million," which he said was "primarily attributable to a decrease in professional and consulting fees." (CFO Parker)
  • Parker reported full-year earnings swing: "The net income for the year-ended December 31, 2025, was $9.2 million, or net income of $0.83 per basic common and $0.82 per diluted common share," compared with "a net loss of $166.5 million or a net loss of $35.21 per basic and diluted common share" in 2024. (CFO Parker)

Q&A

  • Unknown Analyst, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about FDA toxicology questions and whether there is "any plausible concern or risk around immunogenicity" given similarity to Xofluza; CEO Dukes: "baloxavir has a clean immunogenicity label" and said the plan is "to repeat some of these assays and submit new assays as well and using Xofluza as an additional control," adding, "there's no reason a priority why we should be any different to Xofluza." (CEO Dukes)
  • Unknown Analyst, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about MHRA risk and whether there could be delays; CEO Dukes: "We actually don't -- we can't really answer that question today" and said MHRA is on "a 30-day clock," adding, "we remain hopeful and optimistic that the MHRA will indeed approve the study as submitted." (CEO Dukes)
  • Unknown Analyst, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked if dosing could shift from monthly to "twice monthly or even once weekly" based on challenge data; CEO Dukes: "No, not at all" and said the company will assess "the degree of protection at 1 week, 2 week, 3 week as well as 4 weeks" before deciding Phase IIb/III dosing frequency. (CEO Dukes)
  • Unknown Analyst, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked what triggers access to the final $30 million tranche; CFO Parker: "The final warrant C, $30 million has an accelerated feature" if the stock trades at "2x the deal price, which was $1.67, then for 30 days consecutively," which would create "a 10-day window to force exercise." (CFO Parker)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused on regulatory and technical risk, pressing on FDA toxicology requirements, MHRA timing uncertainty, and dosing flexibility, with questions framed around "plausible concern or risk" and potential "delays." (Unknown Analyst, Cantor Fitzgerald)
  • Management tone was slightly positive but conditional on regulators and data, using phrases such as "we remain hopeful and optimistic" and "we are actively engaging with the FDA," while also acknowledging limits: "we can't really answer that question today." (CEO Dukes)
  • A prior-quarter sentiment comparison was not possible because the previous-quarter transcript was not available.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • A quarter-over-quarter comparison was not possible because the previous-quarter transcript was not available.

Risks and Concerns

  • Dukes identified a key regulatory risk in the U.S.: "our IND filing has been placed on clinical hold due to concerns for the toxicology data package," with mitigation described as repeating assays and adding controls: "repeat some of these assays" and "using Xofluza as an additional control." (CEO Dukes)
  • Dukes flagged uncertainty in U.K. regulatory outcomes: "these regulatory agents come to different conclusions based on identical toxicology packages submitted," while noting MHRA review timing via "a 30-day clock." (CEO Dukes)
  • Parker described financing structure and potential dilution/trigger dynamics via warrants, including the $30 million warrant’s acceleration if the stock trades at "2x the deal price" ($1.67) for "30 days consecutively." (CFO Parker)

Final Takeaway

Management centered the update on a $60 million private financing designed to fund tivoxavir marboxil development, with a near-term plan to complete an Australian bridging study and initiate a U.K. flu prevention human challenge trial in summer 2026, while working to resolve an FDA clinical hold tied to toxicology. The company also emphasized that cash plus potential warrant proceeds, if fully exercised, is expected to support planned expenses into Q1 2027, alongside ongoing efforts to position tivoxavir for prophylaxis and potential pandemic preparedness use cases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UK investor Fred Blackford builds $500M stake in ByteDance, FT reports
data.description:
Fred Blackford, who heads up Future Positive Global, started buying stock in TikTok's Chinese parent ByteDance (BDNCE) on the secondary market last year, betting that the company is undervalued compared with its Silicon Valley rivals, the Financial Times reported.

“There was a real mismatch between what a high-quality business we knew it to be versus what it was priced at,” Blackford told FT.

Future Positive intends to keep building its ByteDance position despite the risk of making big bets on Chinese tech companies at a time of strained relations between Washington and Beijing.

Future Positive constructed its stake through a “handful” of transactions, dealing with both US and international investors: Blackford.

ByteDance itself was not involved in the process and did not respond to a request for comment on its new investor, the report said.

data.title: Baidu HK shares jump 8.5%, extend gains on strong cloud momentum
data.description:
Baidu’s Hong Kong-listed shares rose as much as 8.5% on Thursday, extending gains into a third session, with investor appetite boosted by signs of strong cloud business momentum.

Baidu’s cloud ranked first among peers in China with a win of 25 projects worth 1.25 billion yuan in the first quarter, according to a report by Shanghai Securities News, citing data from a third-party industry agency.

In total, major domestic cloud providers won 85 projects, with disclosed contract amounts reaching approximately 1.65 billion yuan.

“For Baidu, AI Cloud Infra revenue is forecast to grow over 40% y/y and expected to beat market consensus at 29% y/y,” Jefferies analysts including Thomas Chong said in a note, as per a Bloomberg report.

Speculation over the IPO progress of Baidu’s (BIDU) chip unit Kunlunxin also helped with the shares rally, Steven Leung, an executive director at Uob Kay Hian said as per the report. Baidu is scheduled to raise fees by about 5% to 30% on a range of its cloud products and services starting from April 18.

The Chinese tech giant's U.S.-listed stock (BIDU) is down 7% YTD.

data.title: Thursday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Jobless Claims

New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time. 

Claims are seen back down at 215K after jumping by 16K to 219K in the previous week.

8:30 AM Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index

The general conditions index from this business outlook survey is a diffusion index of manufacturing conditions within the Philadelphia Federal Reserve district.

The index was seen down in April but still solidly in expansion at 12.0 versus 18.1 in March.

8:35 AM John Williams Speaks

New York Federal Reserve Bank President John Williams gives a keynote speech before the FHLBNY 2026 Member Symposium organized by the Federal Home Loan Bank of New York.

9:15 AM Industrial Production

The Federal Reserve's monthly index of industrial production and the related capacity indexes and capacity utilization rates cover manufacturing, mining, and electric and gas utilities.

A modest increase in industrial output is expected at 0.1 percent, with manufacturing up 0.3 percent and capacity utilization flat at 76.3 percent for March.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report 

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country.

10:35 AM Stephen Miran Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Stephen Miran participates in a conversation before the Washington Economic Festival: "Global Macro Sessions". 

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed's balance sheet is a weekly report presenting a consolidated balance sheet for all 12 Reserve Banks that lists factors supplying reserves into the banking system and factors absorbing reserves from the system.

data.title: Crude oil steady on signs U.S., Iran may extend ceasefire, restart talks
data.description:
Crude oil futures finished nearly unchanged Wednesday, as traders awaited more clarity about a potential second round of U.S.-Iran negotiations, a possible extension of the ceasefire, and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Both sides reportedly have agreed in principle to meet, although no firm decision has been made on a date and venue.

The two sides also reportedly are considering a two-week ceasefire extension to allow more time to negotiate a peace deal, and Iran is said to have proposed allowing ships to ‌sail freely through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack.

The U.S. military said its blockade of shipping leaving Iranian ports has completely halted trade going in and out of the country by sea.

Expectations for further talks have been bearish for oil, while U.S. inventory data for last week was on the bullish side, with draws in crude, gasoline, and diesel stocks.

The Energy Information Administration reported U.S. commercial crude oil stocks fell by 900K barrels last week after seven straight weeks of increases, while releases from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve rose to 4.1M barrels.

U.S. crude exports rose by 1.1M bbl/day to 5.2M bbl/day, and imports fell by 1M bbl/day, according to the EIA.

"We have finally started to see some proper oil stock draws, including SPR in the mix," Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities said in a note. "It makes perfect sense that U.S. oil is being drawn upon globally, but U.S. prices will eventually, perhaps quickly, rise to defend their own inventory."

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery added one cent to $91.29/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery edged up 0.1% to $94.93/bbl.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) edged higher to snap a five-session losing streak, with the front-month Nymex May contract settling up 0.4% at $2.61/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Vivos targets cash flow positive operations by end of 2026 as it scales SO teams with $500,000+ monthly collections potential
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Vivos Therapeutics (VVOS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • “2025 was a pivotal year for Vivos,” said (Co-Founder, Chairman of the Board & CEO R. Kirk Huntsman), adding that the company “proved our core thesis around patient demand and preference for our Vivos method” and said the SCN rollout “has proven to be everything we expected it to be.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) tied the operating model shift directly to profitability goals, saying “recently announced significant cost savings initiatives and strengthened capital structure have now positioned Vivos to drive higher top line growth, better contribution margins and a clear path forward towards our goal of cash flow positive operations by the end of this year.”
  • (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Bradford Amman) said the “full year 2025 revenue increase of $2.4 million or 16%” was driven by “an increase of approximately $4.8 million in sleep testing services and an increase of approximately $2.2 million of revenue generated from the treatment to patients launched at 2 of SCN’s 7 sleep center locations,” while noting the pivot “fully expected revenue from these legacy programs to decline.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) emphasized capacity and payer access as near-term gating items, saying the SCN limiting constraints were “insufficient physical space,” “an adequate number of providers and staff,” and “customary issues with third-party payer credentialing,” while adding SCN is now receiving “notices of in-network status with a number of commercial health insurance payers, along with participating status with Medicare.”

Outlook

  • (CEO Huntsman) framed near-term ramp timing around staffing and payer access: “the full impact of having replaced these doctors… will start to be seen towards the end of Q1 and then into Q2,” and added that payer participation “just started to fold into the revenue productivity stream in the latter part of Q1… but most of the impact is going to be in Q2 forward.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) outlined unit economics targets tied to staffing deployment: “each SO team could potentially generate collections well in excess of $500,000 per month… with contribution margins well above 50%,” and separately said Nevada revenue per case “averages just under $5,000 with contribution margins above 50%.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) described the expansion path as more capital-efficient affiliations, stating “typical capital outlays for an affiliation are under $1 million… affiliations typically preserve about 75% to 80% of the economics for the company.”

Financial Results

  • (CFO Amman) reported 2025 gross profit of “$10.5 million” vs. “$9 million” in 2024, with gross margin “constant at 60%” in both years, while cost of sales rose “approximately $900,000 or 15% to $6.9 million” on higher diagnostic services and staffing for sleep center affiliations.
  • (CFO Amman) said operating expenses rose to “$30.4 million” from “$20.2 million,” driven by G&A of “$27.7 million” including “$6.7 million in costs associated with running SCN operations,” plus “approximately $1.6 million related to professional fees, most of which were onetime expenses.”
  • (CFO Amman) said net loss increased to “$21.2 million,” and cash usage included “net cash used in operating activities… approximately $15.3 million” vs. “$12.7 million” in 2024; year-end cash was “approximately $2 million.”
  • (CFO Amman) highlighted balance sheet pressure and funding actions: total liabilities were “approximately $26.7 million” vs. “$7.3 million” a year earlier, and the company raised “$4.6 million” in gross proceeds from a January 16, 2026 warrant inducement and “$2.25 million” on April 7, 2026 from New Seneca Partners, while noting the need to “continue to augment with additional equity financing… to stay in compliance with NASDAQ’s minimum stockholders’ equity requirement.”

Q&A

  • Scott Henry, Alliance Global Partners: “you did about $6.8 million in Q3… dropped to $3.8 million in Q4… what happened in Q4… [and] your thoughts on Q1?” (CEO Huntsman) answered, “our new model is highly dependent upon total doctor days,” and said Q4 was impacted because “we lost a few providers that we didn’t expect to lose,” adding that in-network payer access began contributing late in Q1 and “most of the impact is going to be in Q2 forward.”
  • Henry, Alliance Global Partners: what revenue level is needed for cash flow positive and what about “$8.3 million in current portion of long-term debt”? (CFO Amman) said that beyond cost actions, “we need to be close to double [the 2025 $17 million] by 2027… on a net income positive basis,” and on debt said “the maturity date on that is in 2026,” adding the company could “roll it over or pay it off.”
  • Robert Sassoon, Water Tower Research: growth in diagnostic/treatment under the new model and lessons from SCN integration? (CFO Amman) said sleep testing service revenue was “about $6 million” in 2025 and treatment revenue was “$2.2 million,” with legacy declines expected to “start to roll off” in 2026; (CEO Huntsman) said lessons included working “in a collaborative manner” with physicians, navigating payers, and said Vivos is “contracting with a national firm” to accelerate in-network participation.
  • Sassoon, Water Tower Research: traction from SoundHealth and long-term growth prospects? (CEO Huntsman) said “it’s still fairly early,” but “we’re having trouble keeping those… units in stock,” and on growth said “we sort of bet the farm here on… SCN… that bet is paying off,” while also citing inbound interest from specialty groups and saying affiliations are “a fraction of what it costs for us to acquire.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on execution volatility and financing, with questions pointing to Q4 revenue drop, cash flow thresholds, and near-term debt maturity, including “what happened in Q4” and whether the debt would be “rolling… over.”
  • Management tone was slightly positive and forward-leaning, using confidence language such as “we feel very, very good,” “we remedied it,” and “we are more optimistic… than we probably have ever been,” while acknowledging an operational surprise: “we got caught a little by surprise in Q4.”
  • Compared with Q3’s “watershed quarter” framing and emphasis on rapid ramp, Q4 commentary added more discussion of provider attrition, payer credentialing, and explicit balance-sheet constraints tied to NASDAQ equity compliance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 emphasized first full quarter post-SCN with revenue “increased 76% to $6.8 million,” while Q4 discussion centered on a sequential decline referenced in Q&A (“dropped to $3.8 million in Q4”) and management attributing it to lost “doctor days” from unexpected provider absences.
  • Q3 highlighted demand outstripping capacity and a credentialing ramp, while Q4 updated that payer participation improved (“in-network status… along with… Medicare”) and that provider redundancy was added to avoid repeat disruptions.
  • Q3 commentary focused on scaling SAMC/SO teams and ramp economics; Q4 reinforced those economics with a quantified claim that each SO team could generate “well in excess of $500,000 per month,” while adding capital structure actions and NASDAQ equity compliance needs.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CEO Huntsman) identified operational bottlenecks as “physical space,” “providers and staff recruiting, training and onboarding,” and “third-party payer credentialing,” and warned revenue sensitivity to staffing: “When a dentist doesn’t show up for a day, it can be… $15,000, $20,000, $30,000 or more of lost production.”
  • (CFO Amman) pointed to liquidity and listing-related constraints, saying additional equity financing is needed “to stay in compliance with NASDAQ’s minimum stockholders’ equity requirement,” alongside debt maturities reclassified as current because “the maturity date on that is in 2026.”

Final Takeaway

Management portrayed 2025 as the first year the SCN-centered MSO/DSO model began to translate into higher diagnostic and treatment revenue, while also acknowledging that the model’s output is tightly linked to provider availability and payer credentialing timelines. Executives emphasized improved in-network coverage and staffing redundancy as key catalysts for a stronger Q1-to-Q2 revenue trajectory, and reiterated a target of cash flow positive operations by the end of 2026, while highlighting near-term financing needs tied to NASDAQ stockholders’ equity compliance and 2026 debt maturities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
